How we work

Most engagements ship in 4–8 weeks. Same four steps every time. Fixed price, no surprise invoices, all IP yours from day one.

Step 1 · Day 0

20 min

We listen. You describe the problem in your words. We ask the questions that matter — current workflow, who owns it, what's been tried, what would shipping mean. Free, no deck, no pitch. If we're not the right team, we'll say so on the call.

You walk away with

Step 2 · Day 1–7

1–2 weeks

Deep discovery — interviews, code/data review, technical risk audit. We come back with a fixed-scope SOW: what's in, what's out, what it costs, what could change it. You sign or you don't. No mystery, no surprise invoices, no scope creep.

Step 3 · Week 1–6

4–6 weeks typical

Senior engineers, working in your repo from week one. Weekly demos — you see real code running, not slides. Bi-weekly retros. Tight feedback loop. We catch direction-changes early when they're cheap.

Step 4 · Week 6+

Production launch, runbook handover, knowledge transfer to your team. Code, prompts, agent configs, and ops docs all transferred to your repository. We're available on hourly retainer if you want us for tuning or extensions, but you don't owe us anything beyond the SOW.

We demo working code at every milestone. If we don't have something to show, that's a status problem we resolve before the meeting — not a slide we hide it behind.

Most fixed-fee overruns come from skipped discovery. We invest 1–2 weeks upfront specifically to avoid that. If we're not confident, we'll say so and propose a paid discovery sprint first.

No juniors learning on your dime. Backed by Alian Software's engineering bench so we don't have to compromise on seniority to meet a price.

content:
Alian AI — Tell us your problem. We'll architect the AI that solves it.

Production AI under real-world security constraints.

Our principles

Least privilege by default

Your keys, your accounts

Audit-traceable AI

No training on your data

Technical controls

Encryption in transit

Encryption at rest

Network controls

Identity + access

Secrets management

Logging + monitoring

Vulnerability management

Backup + recovery

Compliance posture

Deployment options

Incident response

Want a deeper security review?

Security

What we do to keep your data, your code, and your AI infrastructure safe. This page is the honest version — what's actually in place, what's in progress, and where the boundaries are.

Engineers get access scoped to the engagement, not the org. Production secrets stay in your accounts. We never need superuser access to ship.

Anthropic, OpenAI, infra, observability — all run on your accounts with your keys. We never proxy or mint credentials on your behalf.

Every prompt, retrieval, tool call, and output logged with reasoning. Auditors get a reproducible trail. We design to that standard from day one.

We use Anthropic and OpenAI's API tiers that exclude customer data from training. We don't fine-tune on your data without an explicit agreement.

TLS 1.2+ everywhere. HSTS on public endpoints. Mutual TLS available for on-prem and edge gateway deployments.

AES-256 on databases, object storage, and backups. Customer-managed keys (CMK / KMS) supported for enterprise engagements.

VPC isolation, private networking between services, IP allowlists for admin paths, WAF on customer-facing surfaces. No public DB endpoints.

SSO via SAML / OIDC. Hardware-key MFA required for engineers with access to client systems. Per-engagement repo and credential scoping.

Doppler / 1Password / Vault on our side. We use your secrets manager when working in your infra. No secrets in source, ever.

Application logs to Langfuse + your SIEM. Anomaly alerts route to on-call. We keep 90 days at full fidelity, then anonymized aggregates.

Dependabot + Renovate on every repo. SCA + SAST in CI. Quarterly internal pen test on shared infrastructure.

Daily encrypted backups with point-in-time recovery (Postgres). Restore drills quarterly. RTO ≤ 4 hours / RPO ≤ 1 hour for retained systems.

Need a specific framework that's not listed? Email security@aliansoftware.net — we'll tell you honestly whether we can meet it.

We deploy where your data residency requirements need us to:

Five steps, in order, every time. We don't improvise during an incident.

We have a packet for your security team — current SOC 2 progress, DPA template, SCCs, incident response runbook, sub-processor list. Email and we'll send it within a business day.

Most security reviews close in under a week.

- Least privilege by default Engineers get access scoped to the engagement, not the org. Production secrets stay in your accounts. We never need superuser access to ship.
- Your keys, your accounts Anthropic, OpenAI, infra, observability — all run on your accounts with your keys. We never proxy or mint credentials on your behalf.
- Audit-traceable AI Every prompt, retrieval, tool call, and output logged with reasoning. Auditors get a reproducible trail. We design to that standard from day one.
- No training on your data We use Anthropic and OpenAI's API tiers that exclude customer data from training. We don't fine-tune on your data without an explicit agreement.

- Encryption in transit TLS 1.2+ everywhere. HSTS on public endpoints. Mutual TLS available for on-prem and edge gateway deployments.
- Encryption at rest AES-256 on databases, object storage, and backups. Customer-managed keys (CMK / KMS) supported for enterprise engagements.
- Network controls VPC isolation, private networking between services, IP allowlists for admin paths, WAF on customer-facing surfaces. No public DB endpoints.
- Identity + access SSO via SAML / OIDC. Hardware-key MFA required for engineers with access to client systems. Per-engagement repo and credential scoping.
- Secrets management Doppler / 1Password / Vault on our side. We use your secrets manager when working in your infra. No secrets in source, ever.
- Logging + monitoring Application logs to Langfuse + your SIEM. Anomaly alerts route to on-call. We keep 90 days at full fidelity, then anonymized aggregates.
- Vulnerability management Dependabot + Renovate on every repo. SCA + SAST in CI. Quarterly internal pen test on shared infrastructure.
- Backup + recovery Daily encrypted backups with point-in-time recovery (Postgres). Restore drills quarterly. RTO ≤ 4 hours / RPO ≤ 1 hour for retained systems.

- Multi-region cloud — AWS / GCP / Azure in your region of choice. Vercel for client-side, your accounts for the rest.
- Hybrid / edge gateway — Real-time data and agent orchestration on-prem (NUC-class hardware), reasoning in the cloud over scoped channels. Used by manufacturing clients.
- Fully on-prem — Open-source models via vLLM on your hardware when even the reasoning loop can't leave your network. Slower iteration, but doable.

- 1 Detection — alerting on our infra and on yours (with permission)
- 2 Triage — on-call engineer paged within 15 minutes during business hours, 1 hour off-hours
- 3 Containment — scoped credentials revoked, blast radius mapped
- 4 Notification — affected clients notified within 24 hours of confirmed material incident
- 5 Postmortem — written within 5 business days, shared with affected clients in full

Framework | Status | Notes
GDPR | Aligned · DPA on file | We sign your DPA or use ours. SCCs included for EU↔non-EU transfers.
India DPDP Act | Aligned · DPA on file | Standard Indian DPDP-aligned DPA template available.
HIPAA | BAA available | For healthcare engagements. Routed through Anthropic / OpenAI enterprise tiers with BAA.
SOC 2 Type II | In progress | We build to SOC 2 controls. Type II audit underway with target completion later this year.
ISO 27001 | Build-to standard | We build deployments to ISO 27001 readiness. Audit is between you and your auditor.
PCI DSS | Out of scope | We don't process card data. Engagements with PCI requirements use compliant payment providers.

content:
Alian AI — Tell us your problem. We'll architect the AI that solves it.

Have a problem worth experimenting on?

Labs

Internal experiments. Some land in client work. Some get shelved. We share them so you can see how we think about the problems we're paid to solve — and how we keep getting better at them.

A single eval harness that scores AI agents across anonymized client deployments — same test cases, different prompts and contexts. Lets us catch drift across the whole portfolio in one place.

Why: Drift is the boring failure mode that kills agents. Centralized eval lets a 6-person team watch 20+ agents responsibly.

Programmatically generate adversarial conversations for support agents — confused users, multi-turn manipulation, off-policy requests. Used to grow eval suites without waiting for production failures.

Why: 20 hand-picked test cases cover the obvious. Synthetic generation extends coverage into the long tail.

End-to-end on-prem AI stack — vLLM for inference, pgvector for retrieval, n8n for orchestration, Langfuse for observability. We deploy variants of this for clients who can't send data to a cloud LLM.

Why: On-prem AI is harder than people admit. Having a reference deployment shortens client engagements meaningfully.

Lightweight TS/Python library that wraps Anthropic + OpenAI calls and emits token cost per conversation, per user, per workflow to your observability tool. We use it in every engagement now.

Why: Clients deserve to know what every conversation cost. Most observability tools don't make that easy.

YOLO + vision-LLM hybrid for low-volume QC — uses YOLO for the common defects (fast, cheap) and a vision-LLM for the uncertain cases (slower, smarter). Pilot running in two plants.

Why: Pure YOLO needs too much labeled data for SMB volumes. Pure VLM is too slow for production lines. Hybrid is the right answer.

Idea: tiny agents that run fully in-browser via WebGPU for prospects who want to test without sending data anywhere. Shelved because the model quality at WebGPU-sized inference isn't good enough yet.

Why: We'll revisit when the local model quality catches up — probably late 2026.

Treating agents like services with contracts. Schema-validated tool calls, OpenAPI-style interface docs, contract tests in CI that fail builds if the agent breaks its interface.

Why: Agents-as-services scales better than agents-as-magic. The boring infrastructure pattern is the right one.

We pilot interesting projects at a discount when the IP we earn is valuable to the rest of our client base. Tell us what you've got.

We use it in every engagement now. Why: Clients deserve to know what every conversation cost. Most observability tools don't make that easy.

Pilot running in two plants. Why: Pure YOLO needs too much labeled data for SMB volumes. Pure VLM is too slow for production lines. Hybrid is the right answer.

Shelved because the model quality at WebGPU-sized inference isn't good enough yet. Why: We'll revisit when the local model quality catches up — probably late 2026.

The boring infrastructure pattern is the right one.

Returns the same single-page PDF we use on Strategy engagement day-1. Zero-dependency, brand-styled, 30 questions across 4 sections.

Accessibility statement

What we aim for

What's working today

What's not perfect yet

Found something?

Tested with

Legal

Last updated: 14 May 2026

We design the Alian AI site to be usable by as many people as possible. Below is an honest read of where we are.

We target conformance with WCAG 2.1 Level AA . Our deeper goal: a site that's pleasant to use with keyboard, screen reader, or low vision — not just one that passes audit checks.

We take a11y reports seriously. Email accessibility@aliansoftware.net with the URL and what you ran into. We'll respond within 5 business days and tell you when (or if) we plan to ship a fix.

- Skip-to-content link at the top of every page, visible on focus.
- Keyboard navigation across nav, search (⌘K), forms, CTAs, and the chat widget.
- Semantic landmarks — every page has a main element with a stable ID, proper heading hierarchy.
- Focus-visible rings on interactive elements with a coral ring that meets contrast.
- Prefers-reduced-motion respected on the typing headline, activity ticker, and most scroll animations.
- Dark mode + light mode , both audited for contrast on text and UI states.
- Form labels on every input. Error states are announced and visible.

- The hero's auto-cycling placeholder swap may distract some users. We have a plan to add a pause-on-focus toggle.
- The activity ticker pauses when the tab is backgrounded but doesn't yet expose a manual pause control.
- Per-page OG images aren't a11y blockers but lack textual alt equivalents in the metadata.
- We haven't run a full SR audit against the chat widget on iOS VoiceOver yet. On the roadmap.

- Keyboard-only navigation on Chrome, Safari, Firefox
- macOS VoiceOver (latest)
- NVDA on Windows 11
- axe-core + Lighthouse a11y audits per release

content:
Alian AI — Tell us your problem. We'll architect the AI that solves it.

How we engineer AI that doesn't break at 11pm.

Six pillars

Exception-first design

Action surface before prompts

Approval-gated writes

Observability from commit one

Eval suite as a permanent system

Boring infrastructure

Four phases · what's actually produced

1 · Discovery (1–2 weeks)

2 · Architecture + scope

3 · Build (3–6 weeks)

4 · Hardening + ship

Five non-negotiables

See the methodology in action.

Methodology

This is deeper than /process — that's the engagement flow. This is the engineering methodology. Six pillars, four phases, five non-negotiables.

Most teams design for the happy path and add guardrails later. We design for the exceptions first — refusal patterns, low-confidence routing, escalation queues. Happy path falls out.

Before any prompt, we list every action the agent can take. Verb-object pairs, schemas, failure modes. Written collaboratively with the human currently doing the workflow.

v1 agents draft; humans approve; system executes. We graduate to auto-execute only after measured trust at scale. Never the reverse.

Every prompt, retrieval, tool call, and output logged with reasoning. Langfuse by default. Replayable traces are the only way to keep agents healthy past launch.

20+ cases at launch, growing weekly with production failures. Replay against every prompt change. Failing the eval blocks merge.

We prefer obvious solutions over clever ones — Postgres over Kafka, REST over GraphQL, hosted-API over fine-tuning. The clever path is reserved for the AI itself, not the plumbing.

Interviews with 8–12 stakeholders, code/data review, technical risk audit. Output: a scoring rubric for the candidate use cases and a draft scope for the leading one.

Artifacts

Pick the v1, pin the architecture, sign the SOW. We commit to the price; you commit to the dependencies (access, sign-off owners, the data we need).

Senior engineers in your repo from week one. Working code at every weekly demo — not slides, not mockups. Bi-weekly retros to catch direction changes early.

Production launch, runbook handover, knowledge transfer. Daily readout for the first week, weekly for the second month. We're around for tuning if you want us.

We've walked away from engagements where a client wanted us to ship without one of these. Not a flex — just clarity about what shipping AI responsibly actually requires.

Five published case studies. Each one shows this methodology applied to a different domain.

- Action surface before prompts Before any prompt, we list every action the agent can take. Verb-object pairs, schemas, failure modes. Written collaboratively with the human currently doing the workflow.
- Approval-gated writes v1 agents draft; humans approve; system executes. We graduate to auto-execute only after measured trust at scale. Never the reverse.
- Observability from commit one Every prompt, retrieval, tool call, and output logged with reasoning. Langfuse by default. Replayable traces are the only way to keep agents healthy past launch.
- Eval suite as a permanent system 20+ cases at launch, growing weekly with production failures. Replay against every prompt change. Failing the eval blocks merge.
- Boring infrastructure We prefer obvious solutions over clever ones — Postgres over Kafka, REST over GraphQL, hosted-API over fine-tuning. The clever path is reserved for the AI itself, not the plumbing.

In scope

Encrypted reports

Security · Responsible disclosure

We treat security reports seriously. Here's the scope, the process, and what you'll hear back.

Subject line: 'Security report: [short title]'. Include reproduction steps, impact, and a single contact email for follow-up.

A human reads every report. We'll send back a tracking ID and an honest first read on severity within one business day.

Critical (auth bypass, data exposure): patched within 48 hours, you're updated daily. High: 7 days. Medium / low: scheduled into the next sprint.

We'd love 30 days before any public write-up — to let us patch and notify affected users. Faster timelines are negotiable in extreme cases.

For sensitive reports, encrypt with our PGP key. Email security@ for the current public key and fingerprint.

- We acknowledge in 24 hours A human reads every report. We'll send back a tracking ID and an honest first read on severity within one business day.
- We triage + remediate Critical (auth bypass, data exposure): patched within 48 hours, you're updated daily. High: 7 days. Medium / low: scheduled into the next sprint.

- OG image generator

- Third-party services we use (Anthropic, OpenAI, Resend, Vercel) — please report to them directly
- Issues that require physical access or social engineering against our team
- Denial-of-service vulnerabilities (we already rate-limit)
- Output of public AI agents being 'wrong' — that's a quality issue, not a security one

- We will not pursue legal action against good-faith researchers who follow this policy.
- We'll credit you publicly (or keep you anonymous — your call).
- Material findings get a thank-you swag pack. Critical findings get a paid bug bounty (case by case — we don't run a public bounty program yet).

Timeline

15 years of Alian Software. Two years of Alian AI. The bets that worked, the patterns that compounded, and the things we now skip.

Started as a generalist enterprise software firm. Manufacturing was the first vertical — clients needed ERP and didn't have it. We built one and shipped it for them.

The custom ERP we'd built for three manufacturing clients became Alian Infinity — a productized platform with multi-tenant deployment. First three clients ported in.

We outgrew 'manufacturing only.' Started shipping SaaS apps for B2B startups, custom ops platforms for logistics firms, finance integrations for ERP clients.

20+ senior engineers in-house. A handful of specialists in ML, computer vision, and data engineering. Reputation as the team that ships under hard constraints.

First Claude-backed agent shipped — an internal ops assistant for a manufacturing client. Worked surprisingly well. Started thinking about AI as a vertical, not a feature.

Created a dedicated AI services arm. Hired two AI/ML engineers, repurposed the strongest LLM-aware generalists. Started taking AI-only engagements.

Shipped the first multi-agent operations system across a manufacturing plant — six specialized agents, 24/7, 31% downtime reduction. Became our flagship case study.

Multi-agent manufacturing · Auto issue resolution with Claude Code · AI ERP · Lead qualification agent · YouTube automation pipeline. Five wins across five verticals.

After running fixed-fee, hourly, and retainer engagements side-by-side, settled on Fixed-fee Sprint as the default. Predictable for clients, profitable for us. Hourly stays as the on-ramp.

This site. Live Problem Solver hero, real-time AI demos, public case studies, transparent pricing. The public face of work we'd been doing privately for years.

content:
Alian AI — Tell us your problem. We'll architect the AI that solves it.

What can go wrong — and how we mitigate.

Hallucination — confidently wrong

Drift — silent quality decay over time

Prompt injection — user manipulating the agent

Cost runaway — token bills surprising leadership

PII / PHI exposure

Auto-execution of irreversible actions

Vendor lock-in to a single model provider

Regulatory + audit exposure

Vendor disappears mid-engagement

Over-automation — losing the human judgment that matters

Want our risk packet for your security review?

Risks + mitigations

The ten risks we actually plan for on every engagement. Honest severity ratings, the mitigations we build in by default, and what's still on you to own.

The model generates plausible-sounding but false content. Especially dangerous in customer-facing or regulated contexts.

How we mitigate

Model behavior shifts as upstream model providers release updates, your data evolves, or edge cases accumulate. Quality dies quietly.

Users craft inputs that make the agent ignore system instructions, leak prompts, or take unintended actions.

Production AI cost compounds quickly. A change that improves quality 10% but triples cost can blow your budget without notice.

Sensitive personal data ends up in prompts, logs, or model training. Compliance, contractual, and ethical consequences.

Agent sends an email, posts to the ledger, refunds a customer, or merges a PR without proper review.

Built around one model's quirks. Provider raises prices, deprecates the model, or has an outage — you have no fallback.

Auditor asks 'show me the AI's reasoning on account 4729 on March 14.' If you can't, you have a problem.

Your AI vendor (us or anyone else) gets acquired, pivots, or shuts down. Your AI is now an orphan.

Automating workflows that should have kept a human in the loop. Customers feel it. Quality decays in subtle ways. Your team loses skill.

Comprehensive document with our threat model, control mappings, and the specific mitigations we'd build for your engagement. Email security@aliansoftware.net and we'll send within a day.

- Citation-required prompting on every fact-bearing response
- Retrieval-augmented generation with hybrid search + reranking
- Refusal patterns when retrieval confidence is below threshold
- Eval suite that measures hallucination rate weekly
- Human review gate on high-stakes outputs (medical, legal, financial)

- Weekly eval-suite replay against the full case library
- Drift alerting on score deltas above a threshold
- Production failures become permanent eval cases
- Named human owner of the eval suite — non-optional in our SOW
- Quarterly drift retrospective with the client

- Input sanitization at the orchestration layer
- Refusal patterns for obvious injection attempts
- Tool-use gating: no agent action without explicit approval workflow
- Logging + alerting on suspected injection attempts
- System prompts that don't contain anything secret you couldn't put on a billboard

- Per-conversation cost telemetry from day one
- Prompt caching for static context (system prompts, schemas)
- Tier-down to smaller models for low-stakes turns
- Cost budgets with daily / weekly alerts
- Eval suite logs cost per case — surfaces cost-quality regressions

- PII detection + redaction at the orchestration layer
- Encryption in transit and at rest, customer-managed keys when required
- Use of enterprise model tiers that exclude data from training
- DPAs with all sub-processors
- Retention policies sized to your obligations, not our defaults

- v1 agents draft; humans approve; system executes — never the reverse
- Allowlist of touchable actions, deny-by-default for everything else
- Explicit confirmation tokens for irreversible operations
- Audit log on every executed action with named approver
- Sandbox environments for any agent that writes code or executes

- Abstract the model behind a thin internal interface
- Run the eval suite against 2+ model providers
- Open-source model fallback for non-critical paths
- Cost-comparison telemetry across providers, refreshed quarterly

- Replayable reasoning traces — every prompt, retrieval, tool call, output
- Logs tied to named users, named systems, unique decision IDs
- Retention sized to your audit window (often years)
- Model approval workflow with named approver per release
- Refusal patterns documented in plain English for the audit team

- Code, prompts, configs in your repos from day one — not ours
- Infrastructure in your accounts, not ours
- Runbooks written for your team, not for our convenience
- Eval suite owned by a named human on your side
- Quarterly handoff drill — can your team operate this without us?

- Default to human-in-loop on anything emotional, contractual, or strategic
- Sample 5% of automated outputs for human QA review weekly
- Cross-train the team — don't atrophy the skills you'd want back
- Sunset clause in every automation: would we be OK if this stopped tomorrow?

title: Alian AI — Tell us your problem. We'll architect the AI that solves it.
section_heading: Alian AI — Tell us your problem. We'll architect the AI that solves it.
content:
Alian AI — Tell us your problem. We'll architect the AI that solves it.

Forks in the road we took the less-obvious way.

Postgres over Kafka for the multi-agent event bus

Vendor shadcn/ui primitives rather than install

Server-Sent Events over WebSockets for streaming

Refusal patterns at the orchestration layer, not in the system prompt

We turn down 1 in 8 engagements we get to discovery on

Eval suites in client repos, not ours

$25/hour as the public starting rate

We hire when demand calls, not on a growth curve

We don't claim AI quality SLAs — we claim audit-trail SLAs

TypeScript on the frontend, Python on the backend, no GraphQL

Want to talk through a decision you're facing?

Non-obvious decisions

Ten decisions where the obvious answer would have been a mistake. Tech choices, engagement structure, hiring cadence. The context, the why, and what we learned.

Decision # 1

Context

When we built the Multi-Agent Manufacturing System, the obvious answer for inter-agent communication was Kafka. We picked Postgres.

Why we made this call

The client already ran Postgres. Their ops team had years of muscle memory with it. Latency requirements were 'sub-second', which Postgres handles trivially. Kafka would have added a 4-week setup tax for zero functional gain.

What we learned

When the obvious answer adds operational complexity without buying performance you need, the boring answer is correct. We default to Postgres for event buses now and only reach for Kafka when message volume genuinely demands it.

Decision # 2

shadcn/ui is the standard React component library for modern Next.js sites. The standard pattern is `npx shadcn add button`. We hand-copied the source into our repo.

We didn't want surprise updates to break component variants when a maintainer changes class names. Vendoring is 50 lines per primitive — trivial to maintain. We get full customization control and zero version-pinning anxiety.

For surface-area components you'll customize heavily, vendoring beats installing. For deeply integrated frameworks (auth, routing), install. The line is 'do I want to customize the API itself?'

Decision # 3

Streaming AI responses in 2026 — Vercel AI SDK uses SSE. Most production AI infra uses WebSockets for 'real-time.' We default to SSE.

SSE is half the complexity. One-way streaming is what AI generation actually needs. Reconnection is built into the protocol. Cleaner mental model for the team and for code reviewers from outside our team.

Use the simpler protocol when the simpler protocol is enough. WebSockets pay off when you need bidirectional, low-latency. AI streaming isn't that.

Decision # 4

The common pattern is to put refusal instructions in the system prompt: 'refuse if X, refuse if Y...' We moved most of those to deterministic checks in code, before the model is even called.

Prompt-based refusal is probabilistic. Code-based refusal is deterministic. For categories like 'never quote pricing,' you don't want a 95% refusal rate — you want 100%. The model decides intent; the code decides allow/deny.

Use the model where it shines (intent classification, nuanced understanding). Don't use it where determinism matters more (categorical refusals, action allowlists).

Decision # 5

Most agencies say yes to every engagement that pays. We say no when the ROI math doesn't support the build, when the data isn't there, or when the named owner doesn't exist.

Fixed-fee economics break if you take on bad-fit work. One unhappy client costs more than five neutral ones in reputation alone. Saying no protects the fixed-fee model's integrity.

Saying no in discovery is the kindest thing you can do for a client who'd otherwise burn $80K on a build that wouldn't ship. The retainer renewal economics make up for the lost top-line.

Decision # 6

Most agencies keep their eval suites internal. We put eval suites in the client's repo from day one.

When we leave, we want the eval suite to keep running. If it lives in our infra, it dies with the engagement. In their repo, with their CI, owned by their named human — it's sticky.

If you want a deliverable to outlive your engagement, put it where the client lives, not where you live. Even at the cost of more setup work upfront.

Decision # 7

Standard practice for offshore AI agencies is to obscure rates and quote per-engagement. We publish the $25/hour rate on the site.

It filters the funnel ruthlessly. Anyone shocked by $25/hour isn't our buyer. Anyone who's been quoted $150-250/hour by a US agency sees us as exceptional value. Public pricing collapses bad-fit conversations early.

Public, specific pricing is a sorting mechanism. It costs a tiny number of high-budget deals we wouldn't have won anyway, and saves dozens of hours of mis-qualified discovery calls.

Decision # 8

Standard agency playbook: hire ahead of pipeline. We hire when client demand commits us to it.

Hiring ahead of demand requires you to win every pitch to feed the bench. That pressure leads to taking bad-fit work. Hiring against signed commitments removes that pressure entirely.

Your hiring cadence is a strategic lever, not just a cost lever. Slower hiring means you can say no more. Saying no more means better client outcomes. Better outcomes mean better referrals. Compounds.

Decision # 9

Clients sometimes ask for 'X% accuracy SLA' on AI features. We won't sign that. We will sign 'every output has a replayable audit trail within Y minutes' and 'every regression caught in production becomes a permanent eval case within Z business days.'

Quality SLAs on probabilistic systems are dishonest. Audit-trail and response-time SLAs on the system around the AI are honest and measurable. Auditors actually prefer the latter.

Build SLAs around the deterministic parts of your AI system, not the probabilistic ones. Auditors agree. Clients adapt to it quickly once you explain why.

Decision # 10

Common pattern: monorepo with GraphQL throughout. We separate cleanly — TS on the browser, Python where AI/ML happens, REST in between.

GraphQL adds complexity that pays off for graph-shaped data with N+1 problems. Our workloads don't have that. REST + structured JSON keeps the team able to read each other's PRs without specialization.

Pick technologies because they fit your shape of work, not because they're trending. Boring is reliable. Reliable wins compound. Compound interest funds the next clever choice when it actually matters.

Book a 20-min call. We'll share our take and the trade-off we'd be watching for. No commitment beyond.

Budget guide

Five cost layers, three example total budgets, and the five places the numbers tend to surprise people. Written for the budget owner who has to justify this internally.

What the engineering team charges to design, build, eval, and ship. Fixed-fee Sprint for clear scope, hourly for fuzzy scope. Includes 1–2 weeks of paid discovery upfront.

What you pay Anthropic, OpenAI, or open-source providers per token. Pay your own accounts; we don't markup. Caching + smaller-model tiering brings this down meaningfully.

Vercel hosting, Postgres (Supabase / Neon), observability (Langfuse), email (Resend). Often under $100/mo for a single-agent workload, can scale up.

Ongoing engineering hours to keep the eval suite running, tune prompts as the product evolves, add new agents. Most clients renew here after the initial sprint.

Your team's hours scoping, reviewing, evaluating, deploying. Plan on 5–15 hours/week from a named human during the sprint, then 2–5 hours/week ongoing.

Quick win

Standup digest, inbox triage, FAQ deflector

Mid-market sprint

Customer-support agent, lead qualifier, ERP query

Enterprise multi-agent

Manufacturing ops, KYC platform, multi-tenant SaaS feature

Rough ranges, not commitments. Real quote depends on your data, integrations, compliance bar, and the specific use case.

If you only budget the build and forget run cost, your AI feature looks profitable for 6 months and underwater after that. We surface the 18-month total in scoping so this doesn't surprise anyone.

An eval suite that ships and then atrophies is the most common cause of AI quality decay. Plan on a named human putting 2–4 hours/week into it — it's not optional.

If the engagement owner is your VP of Eng, that's $300/hr loaded cost. 10 hours/week for 6 weeks is $18K in attention cost. Plan for it.

Common surprise: a few power users dominate cost. Cost per active user can be highly skewed. We surface this in observability from day one so you can manage it.

Once you see the agent working, you'll want to do more with it. That's healthy. Add it as a separate add-on, quoted up front, not folded silently into the sprint.

20-min discovery call — we'll come back within a business day with a written budget envelope for what you're describing.

content:
Alian AI — Tell us your problem. We'll architect the AI that solves it.

Why we built Alian AI.

The thesis

The strategy

What we won't do

Where we're going

Talk to us.

Founder letter · May 2026

In 2024, we kept seeing the same pattern: companies wanted to ship AI. They had budget. They had a use case. They could not find a partner who'd ship it for them in production. Not a demo. Production.

The big consultancies sold strategy decks at $500K+. The boutique AI agencies in SF and London quoted $250/hour for senior engineers who'd never shipped an agent through a compliance review. The in-house path required 6–9 months of hiring before the first line of code.

We've been shipping enterprise software at Alian Software for 15 years. We know what production-grade looks like — audit trails, eval suites, refusal patterns, observability. We've been building these into AI features for our ERP since 2023. In 2024, we realized: this skill set is rarer than it should be, and the market is paying a premium for it.

So we spun up Alian AI. Not a separate company. A division. Same hiring bar, same code-review process, same engineering bench. Just focused on AI engagements.

Three things are true at once in 2026, and they compound:

We compete on engineering output per dollar. Senior engineers, fixed-fee discipline, public pricing starting at $25/hour, all code and IP transferred to the client on day one. No vendor lock-in disguised as "our framework."

We don't compete on time-zone proximity to US/EU clients. We compete on speed, clarity, and engineering quality. Most of our clients are 8+ hours away from us. The work happens because the output is good enough that the timezone math is irrelevant.

We hire when client demand commits us to it, not when investors expect headcount growth. Slower hiring means we can say no more. Saying no more means better client outcomes. Better outcomes compound. The retainer renewal economics make this math work.

We won't sell strategy decks without code attached. We won't ship agents that take irreversible action without human approval. We won't auto-execute on the basis of "AI accuracy." We won't take engagements where the data isn't there, the named owner isn't there, or the ROI math doesn't pencil.

About 1 in 8 engagements that get to our discovery call don't proceed past it. That's not a flex. It's the only way fixed-fee economics work long-term. Saying yes to everything that pays is how agencies die slowly.

The site you're on is a deliberate artifact. Most agency sites hide pricing, hide methodology, hide the scoping process. We're putting all of it out in the open — pricing, eval-suite templates, sample SOWs, the principles we hold to. Anyone who reads this site for two hours could write a competent first pitch in our voice.

That's intentional. The skill is in the engineering, not in the marketing veneer. If a competitor can replicate our positioning by reading our site, they should — and the market wins. Our edge is what we ship, not what we hide.

If you've read this far, you're probably someone we'd want to work with. Book a 20-min call. We'll tell you honestly whether we're the right team.

— The Alian AI team

20 min. No deck. We'll tell you what's possible and what isn't.

- Cost per token dropped 90% in two years. Use cases that didn't pencil in 2023 pencil now.
- The production engineering scaffolding (eval suites, observability, refusal patterns, MCP) matured into table stakes. Teams that haven't internalized them ship demos that decay.

content:
Alian AI — Tell us your problem. We'll architect the AI that solves it.

When we're not the right team.

You need 8+ hours of daily timezone overlap

You want strategy decks, not code

Your use case is < $20K

You're building a foundation model

You need fully on-prem with no cloud touchpoints

Your problem isn't an AI problem

You want a content-generation factory with no human review

You haven't shipped any AI yet AND want a 10-use-case roadmap

You won't share read-only access to your data

Timing reasons we'd defer

None of these you? Let's talk.

Why not us

Nine situations where another vendor, an in-house build, or a different approach is the better call. We turn down about 1 in 8 engagements at discovery — these are the patterns we see in those 'no's.

We're based in India. Most of our clients are in the US, EU, or ANZ — that's 3–4 hours of daily overlap, max. If your engagement requires the engineer to be online during your full workday, a local agency or in-house hire is the right call.

Try instead: US/EU agencies (see /vs/agency for the comparison) or local in-house.

We can do strategy phases. We do them because they end in shippable artifacts, not slides. If you genuinely want a 90-page strategy deck with no production deliverable attached, Big 4 consultancies are better at that than we are.

Try instead: Big 4 / Tier-1 consultancy. See /vs/consultancy.

Below $20K, the engagement-overhead math doesn't work. You'd be paying for our coordination cost, not output. Try a hourly engagement starting at 20 hours, or build it yourself with one of our free templates.

Try instead: Hourly engagement (/pricing/hourly), or our free /templates.

We build production AI on top of foundation models. We don't train foundation models. If you're building one from scratch — pretraining, RLHF, distillation pipelines — you want a research-heavy team, not a delivery agency.

Try instead: Anthropic / OpenAI / open-source research labs.

We can deploy edge gateways + cloud reasoning for hybrid. We can run open-source models on your hardware for partial on-prem. We don't operate fully air-gapped environments — that's a specialized capability we don't have.

Try instead: Defense / regulated industry specialists with air-gap experience.

If your actual problem is a process problem, a data-quality problem, or a hiring problem, AI won't solve it — it'll usually make it worse by adding a new failure mode. We'd rather you not buy AI from us than buy AI and discover the underlying issue.

Try instead: Process consulting, data engineering, or hiring help.

We won't build agents that publish content without humans approving. Two reasons: quality decays without it, and brand damage from one bad output undoes months of compound value. If you're set on fully unattended generation at scale, we're not the team.

Try instead: Marketing-tech vendors who specialize in content-at-scale tradeoffs.

We'd rather you ship one use case and then talk about the second. Companies that try to scope 10 use cases at once typically ship none. The strategy phase is fine; the 10-use-case sprint isn't.

Try instead: Start with /quick-wins. Then /pricing/sprint for the first build.

We can't scope honestly without seeing the shape of your data. We sign NDAs, we work in your accounts, we don't take data home. But if 'no access' is the constraint, we can't help you and shouldn't pretend we can.

Try instead: Pre-purchase consulting that works from screenshots and walkthroughs.

Maybe later, not now

Pre-PMF startups with no data, no customers, no clear use case. Wait until you have something measurable. Then come back.

Companies in active acquisition. Wait for the deal to close. AI integration during M&A is a known nightmare.

Companies that just had a leadership change at the C-level. Wait for the new exec to settle on strategy. Building AI for a strategy that's about to change is wasted money.

If you read this list and we didn't disqualify ourselves, the math probably works. Book the call.

title: Production agent design · Playbook · Alian AI
section_heading: Production agent design · Playbook · Alian AI
content:
Production agent design · Playbook · Alian AI

Production agent design

1. Define the action surface first

2. Wrap every write action in approval

3. Build the escalation path before the happy path

4. Observability is non-optional

5. Eval before launch, eval after launch

Want this shipped for your team?

Other playbooks

How to design agents that ship — guardrails, escalation, observability.

Most teams stall agents at the demo stage because they design for the happy path and figure out exceptions later. We design exception-first. This is the playbook we use on every agent engagement.

Before writing prompts, list every action the agent can take. Most teams skip this and inherit a sprawling tool list as it grows. Bad outcome.

Build the list collaboratively with the human currently doing the workflow. They'll surface actions you'd miss — and reject ones you'd over-include.

v1 agents don't take write actions autonomously. They draft, request approval, then execute. Period.

Approval can be inline (chat confirmation), routed to a queue, or auto-approved against a policy — but it's explicit. The agent's success metric is the approve-rate, not the auto-execute count.

Every agent has a 'I don't know — route to a human' branch. Building that first forces clarity on what the agent should refuse, what counts as ambiguous, and where humans queue up.

Confidence thresholds matter. We tune them after seeing the first 100 production conversations, not before.

Every prompt, retrieval, tool call, and output gets logged with reasoning. We use Langfuse by default. You don't need fancy dashboards — you need replayable traces when something goes sideways.

Weekly review cadence with the operations owner. We've never run an agent without one and we've never plan to.

20+ test cases in the eval suite from week one. Replay against every prompt change. We don't ship prompt edits without a green eval.

After launch, the eval suite grows weekly with cases from production we want to never fail again.

Common failure mode

Teams build a generic 'AI assistant' that does too many things. Specialized > general for v1. One workflow, done well.

Common skip

Building agents without observability. You'll regret it the first time something breaks at 11pm on Saturday.

We bring this playbook to every engagement. 20-min call gets you a scope.

- Each has an explicit input schema and a clear failure mode

title: RAG that actually grounds · Playbook · Alian AI
section_heading: RAG that actually grounds · Playbook · Alian AI
content:
RAG that actually grounds · Playbook · Alian AI

RAG that actually grounds

1. Hybrid search beats vector-only

2. Reranking is the cheapest precision lift

3. Chunk smartly, not uniformly

4. Citation-required prompting

5. Refusal patterns

Want this shipped for your team?

Other playbooks

Retrieval, reranking, citations, refusal. The pattern that stops hallucination.

Most RAG demos hallucinate within 10 minutes of real use. Production RAG doesn't. The difference is process: hybrid search, reranking, citation-required prompts, and refusal patterns. We ship this on every chatbot engagement.

Vector search misses exact-match queries — names, error codes, SKU numbers. Keyword search misses semantic intent. Hybrid (BM25 + vector) wins.

We score both, normalize, weight, and merge. The weight is tuned per domain. Most projects land 70/30 vector/BM25.

Retrieve top 50 candidates. Rerank to top 5 with Cohere Rerank or a cross-encoder. The model only sees the top 5.

Adds ~150ms latency. Removes most retrieval misses. We've never shipped production RAG without reranking and don't plan to.

Fixed-size chunking is fine for v1 but you'll hit walls. For docs, chunk by heading. For codebases, chunk by function. For conversations, chunk by turn.

Always include the chunk's parent heading or context in the embedded text. Otherwise the model retrieves text out of context.

System prompt instructs: cite the source for every fact. Refuse to answer if you can't cite. Users see citations as inline footnote links.

Hallucination drops 80%+ with citation-required prompting. The rest is the eval suite catching the long tail.

If retrieval confidence is low, refuse and route. Don't make it up. The agent that says 'I don't know — let me get someone' beats the agent that confidently fabricates.

We tune this with a confidence score on retrieval + an LLM-as-judge confidence on the generated answer. Threshold-based refusal triggers the escalation path.

Common failure mode

Skipping reranking 'to keep it simple'. The model retrieves the wrong chunks, hallucinates, and you blame RAG.

Common skip

Documenting your refusal patterns. If your QA team can't list them, your users will find them.

We bring this playbook to every engagement. 20-min call gets you a scope.

title: The eval suite you actually keep running · Playbook · Alian AI
section_heading: The eval suite you actually keep running · Playbook · Alian AI
content:
The eval suite you actually keep running · Playbook · Alian AI

The eval suite you actually keep running

1. Start with 20 cases. Not 200.

2. Score what matters

3. Run weekly, gate releases

4. Grow the suite from production

5. LLM-as-judge, then humans on the close calls

Want this shipped for your team?

Other playbooks

20 test cases, replayed weekly, evolving with production. The minimum for shipping.

An eval suite isn't optional. It's the system that tells you whether your production AI is still doing what it did on launch day. Without it, you ship a great demo and watch it silently degrade. This is the eval pattern we build into every engagement.

20 hand-picked cases beat 200 synthetic ones. The 20 cover happy path, common edge cases, and the patterns you can't afford to break.

We collect them from real conversations (anonymized), pre-launch interviews, and team brainstorming. Each case has expected behavior, not just expected output.

Hallucination rate (LLM-as-judge against ground truth). Refusal correctness (did the agent refuse when it should have?). Tool-call accuracy. Citation correctness.

Aggregate scores are useful, but per-case scoring catches the cases where averages hide real failures.

Eval runs in CI on every prompt change. Failing the eval blocks merge. Engineers learn fast that prompts have a regression risk.

Outside CI, we run the full suite weekly against production. Drift over time shows up here before it shows up in user complaints.

Every failure mode you catch in production becomes a permanent eval case. The suite grows 2-5 cases per week in active development.

Anonymize aggressively before adding production cases — strip PII, names, account numbers.

Have a strong model grade outputs against your criteria. Score 80%+ of cases automatically.

The close calls and the failures go to humans. That's where your tuning judgment lives.

Common failure mode

Treating evals as one-time. They're a permanent system, not a launch checklist.

Common skip

Not having an owner for the eval suite. It atrophies in weeks without one.

We bring this playbook to every engagement. 20-min call gets you a scope.

title: Agent · AI glossary · Alian AI
section_heading: Agent · AI glossary · Alian AI
content:
Agent · AI glossary · Alian AI

Agent

The longer version

Related terms

Want to talk about how this applies to your stack?

More terms

AI glossary

An AI system that runs a workflow — fetches data, calls tools, decides between branches, escalates to humans when its confidence is low. Different from a chatbot (which answers questions) in that an agent takes actions.

The defining feature of an agent vs a chatbot is action. Chatbots answer; agents do. In production, agents pair a language model with: (a) a tool-use loop that lets the model call external functions, (b) a memory layer for multi-step context, (c) explicit guardrails on which actions are allowed without human approval, and (d) escalation paths when confidence drops. We design agents exception-first — the refusal cases, escalation queues, and failure modes get scoped before the happy path. See /playbooks/agent-design for the full pattern.

Agentic workflow

A multi-step process where one or more AI agents coordinate to achieve an outcome. Often involves planning, tool use, retrieval, and human-in-loop checkpoints.

Guardrails

Constraints that limit what an agent can do — what files it can touch, what actions need human approval, what topics it refuses. Differentiates production AI from a demo.

- Agentic workflow A multi-step process where one or more AI agents coordinate to achieve an outcome. Often involves planning, tool use, retrieval, and human-in-loop checkpoints.
- Guardrails Constraints that limit what an agent can do — what files it can touch, what actions need human approval, what topics it refuses. Differentiates production AI from a demo.

content:
Cache (prompt caching) · AI glossary · Alian AI

Cache (prompt caching)

The longer version

Want to talk about how this applies to your stack?

More terms

AI glossary

Reusing the encoded representation of a long, static prompt prefix across many requests. Cuts cost 50-90% on use cases with large shared context (schemas, knowledge bases, instructions).

Anthropic's prompt cache (and OpenAI's structured equivalents) lets you pay for the long static parts of your prompt — system instructions, schemas, large reference docs — once per cache TTL, then ~10% of normal input rate on subsequent reads. For RAG over a large knowledge base with a stable system prompt, this is a 5–10x cost reduction. We surface cache hit rate in observability so it's measurable, not assumed.

title: Citations / grounding · AI glossary · Alian AI
section_heading: Citations / grounding · AI glossary · Alian AI
content:
Citations / grounding · AI glossary · Alian AI

Citations / grounding

The longer version

Related terms

Want to talk about how this applies to your stack?

More terms

AI glossary

Requiring the AI to cite the source of each claim it makes from a retrieved document. The single most effective tool against hallucination in RAG systems.

The pattern: numbered chunks in the retrieved context, system prompt requires inline citations like [1] [2] for every factual claim, refusal when no source supports an answer. Hallucination rate drops 80%+ with this pattern in our deployments. See /templates/rag-citation-prompt for the exact prompt we use.

RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)

Fetching relevant documents from a database first, then asking the model to answer using only those documents. The default architecture for grounded chatbots and knowledge assistants.

Hallucination

When a model generates plausible-sounding but false content. Mitigated by RAG with citations, refusal patterns, retrieval-confidence thresholds, and eval coverage. Not zero, never zero.

Eval / evaluation suite · AI glossary · Alian AI

Eval / evaluation suite

The longer version

Related terms

Want to talk about how this applies to your stack?

More terms

AI glossary

A set of test cases run weekly against an AI system to catch quality regressions before users do. Non-optional for production. We build one for every shipped system.

20+ test cases at launch, growing weekly with production failures. Each case has expected behavior (not just expected output), scoring rubric, and pass threshold. Run in CI on every prompt change. Failing the eval blocks merge. Named owner on the client side. See /playbooks/eval-suite for the full pattern.

LLM-as-judge

Using a strong model to evaluate the outputs of another model against your criteria. Used for eval suites at scale when human grading isn't tractable.

Fetching relevant documents from a database first, then asking the model to answer using only those documents. The default architecture for grounded chatbots and knowledge assistants.

Production RAG isn't just 'fetch chunks and stuff them in the prompt.' Reranking

Running retrieved candidates through a second model to reorder by relevance. Top-50 from vector search reranked to top-5 dramatically improves precision. We use Cohere Rerank.
